labels: To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a fluid, rhythmic motion rather than jerky, segmented movements. You are currently hitting the ball with mostly arm strength; instead, prioritize a full "trophy pose" where your elbow is high and your racket drops behind your back, allowing you to use your legs and core rotation to generate power and maximize your reach at the contact point.

The beginner’s serve lacks a proper ball toss, causing him to hit the ball directly from a static position rather than utilizing the kinetic chain. To improve, focus on a consistent, high-reaching ball toss in front of your body to allow for a full arm extension and a fluid weight transfer into the court. By leading with your legs and rotating your core, you will generate the necessary power and reach that the expert demonstrates with his controlled, rhythmic service motion.

In the first video, your movement is somewhat disjointed; focus on improving your coordination by initiating the racket drop and the trophy position simultaneously with your leg drive. Aim for a more fluid kinetic chain, ensuring that your racket head accelerates consistently through the contact point rather than stopping mid-motion. Finally, practice a more aggressive, synchronized toss and weight transfer to generate the power and reach observed in the expert's serve.

The beginner’s serve lacks a proper full-arm reach and fluid weight transfer, leading to a "pushing" motion rather than an explosive strike. To improve, focus on starting with a more relaxed grip to allow for a full "trophy pose" reach, and ensure you shift your weight forward into the court during the swing to generate natural power rather than relying solely on your arm.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a fluid, full motion by extending your arm upward toward the ball rather than cutting the swing short. You should also work on your ball toss consistency; in the video, your toss is too low and close to your body, which prevents you from fully rotating your torso and accessing the "trophy position" needed for maximum power and reach.

While your basic movement is on the right track, your toss and weight transfer need significant refinement to improve your power and consistency. Focus on keeping your tossing arm extended longer and lifting it toward the ceiling, which will help you achieve a more vertical reach at contact rather than hitting the ball out in front of your body prematurely. Additionally, work on pushing off your back foot more aggressively to shift your weight forward into the court, as you are currently staying too stagnant through the impact zone.

Your toss is currently inconsistent, which is forcing you to reach behind your body rather than extending fully through the ball. To improve, focus on releasing the ball at eye level with a straighter arm to ensure a more stable contact point in front of you. Once your toss is stabilized, aim to initiate your swing with a more fluid upward reach to generate the power and topspin characteristic of a high-level serve.

Your serve lacks the necessary fluid coil and explosive hip rotation seen in the expert’s motion; you are currently moving your arms in isolation rather than utilizing your entire body to generate power. Focus on a deeper, more synchronized shoulder turn during your wind-up and ensure your weight shifts forward into the ball to achieve a proper kinetic chain. Establishing this full-body coordination will transform your serve from a simple arm movement into a powerful, efficient stroke.

Your current serve mechanics are too rigid, lacking the fluid kinetic chain required for power and consistency. Focus on your trophy pose by getting your elbow higher and more relaxed, and ensure you use a full, rhythmic arc rather than just lifting the racket. By smoothing out your transition from the setup to the ball contact, you will generate significantly more racket-head speed and better control.

The beginner is missing the essential "trophy pose" and fluid kinetic chain required for a powerful serve; instead of fully extending the arm and rotating the torso, the motion is mostly arm-driven. To improve, focus on starting with a more relaxed grip and emphasize a full, high reach to initiate the swing, which will help create the proper overhead arc and ball contact point demonstrated by the expert.

As a coach, I can see you have the right basic idea, but you are currently missing the fluid, continuous motion that generates power. Your serve is too segmented; focus on a fluid "trophy pose" where your arm and racket move together in a synchronized arc, rather than pausing mid-motion. Additionally, ensure you are fully extending your arm upward to meet the ball at the highest possible point, as this will improve both your accuracy and your ability to drive the ball downward into the service box.

Your current technique lacks the proper kinetic chain; you need to focus on a full, fluid wind-up that utilizes your legs to generate upward power rather than just swinging with your arm. Practice starting your motion with a deeper knee bend and an explosive toss, ensuring your racket head "drops" behind your back before accelerating upward into the ball to achieve a more consistent and powerful flat serve.

The beginner in the first video needs to focus on a more fluid, overhead motion rather than stopping their arm mid-swing. To generate more power and consistency, try to reach higher toward the ball at the peak of your toss and follow through completely across your body, rather than letting the racket drop abruptly after contact.

The beginner’s toss is too low and lacks the full upward extension needed to contact the ball at the highest possible point, which causes them to hit the ball flat rather than driving through it. To improve, focus on releasing the ball higher and reaching fully toward the sky with your hitting arm to create the necessary leverage and downward angle for a more effective serve. Additionally, try to incorporate more fluid knee flexion and rotation into your motion to generate power from your legs rather than relying solely on your arm.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a fuller, more fluid kinetic chain by starting your swing with a deeper knee bend and a more pronounced shoulder turn. Additionally, aim to reach higher at the point of contact to ensure you are striking the ball at the peak of your extension, rather than hitting it while the racquet is still traveling at an upward angle.

The beginner's toss is inconsistent and lacks verticality, causing them to reach for the ball awkwardly rather than meeting it at the peak of their extension. To improve, focus on releasing the ball higher from a straighter arm to ensure a fluid, upward swing path that maximizes power and contact point height.

To improve your flat serve, you need to focus on your toss and your arm extension. Your toss should be placed slightly in front of your body to allow for better weight transfer, and you should aim for full extension of your hitting arm at the point of contact rather than pulling your elbow in early. Practicing a fluid, rhythmic motion will help you generate the necessary power and consistent ball flight seen in the expert's demonstration.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and lacks the necessary vertical height to allow for a full arm extension at contact, which forces you to strike the ball prematurely. To improve your flat serve, focus on releasing the ball higher and timing your upward swing so that your arm is fully extended at the moment of impact. Additionally, try to incorporate a more fluid weight transfer by pushing forward into the court rather than staying static, which will help generate the power and racquet-head speed seen in the second video.

To improve your serve, focus on your service motion; while the expert uses a fluid, full-body rotation to generate power, your motion is currently too segmented and lacks a proper trophy pose. Practice keeping your tossing arm extended longer and work on a smoother transition into your swing, ensuring you lead with your elbow to create a more efficient racket-head speed.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a fluid, full-range motion rather than an abbreviated one; your current technique lacks the necessary shoulder rotation and upward reach required to generate power. Aim to fully extend your arm toward the point of contact at the apex of your toss, mirroring the expert's pronounced follow-through, which will help you transition from a simple "tap" to a dynamic, high-velocity serve.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and lands too far behind your body, which forces you to arch your back awkwardly and limits your power. Focus on keeping your tossing arm extended longer and releasing the ball at eye level, so you can contact it while reaching fully upward rather than falling backward. Additionally, strive to lead with the edge of the racquet to ensure you are hitting through the ball with a fluid, upward motion rather than "chopping" at it.

To improve your flat serve, you must develop a consistent and full service motion that includes a proper trophy position, as your current swing is abbreviated and lacks the necessary vertical lift. Focus on extending your arm upward toward the contact point rather than swinging across your body, which will help you achieve the clean, forward-driven contact characteristic of the expert's technique.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a more fluid and continuous "trophy pose" by lifting your racquet and tossing arm in better synchronization. Currently, your motion is segmented; try to initiate the forward swing by driving your legs upward to generate power from your core rather than just relying on your arm strength. Integrating a smoother, more rhythmic kinetic chain will help you achieve the effortless extension seen in the expert's serve.

To improve your flat serve, you need to focus on extending your range of motion; while the expert utilizes a full, fluid overhead swing, your current motion is too abbreviated and lacks the necessary racquet head speed. Try to lead with your elbow and allow for a more pronounced "trophy pose" at the top of your backswing to generate the momentum required for a powerful, flat strike.

In the first video, your toss and swing path are inconsistent because your arm is rushing through the motion, causing you to lose control of the racket face. Focus on a controlled, rhythmic tossing arm that stays extended longer, and ensure your racket drops deeper behind your back before initiating the upward "throw" toward the ball to generate the fluid, whip-like acceleration shown by the expert.

Your toss needs to be more consistent and higher to allow for a full, fluid swing path; currently, you are rushing the motion because the ball is too low. Focus on extending your tossing arm fully toward the ceiling and keeping your racket head path consistent until the point of contact to generate more power and accuracy.

To improve your serve, focus on your toss and your body rotation. Currently, your toss is inconsistent, which forces your arm to compensate; try to release the ball consistently at eye level to establish a steady contact point. Additionally, engage your core and rotate your hips more fully through the swing to generate power from your legs rather than relying solely on your arm.

Your serve lacks the necessary fluid coil and full extension required to generate power. Focus on a deeper knee bend and a more pronounced shoulder turn to build kinetic energy, then ensure you reach fully upward to contact the ball at the highest possible point rather than cutting your follow-through short.

Your service motion lacks a full coil and proper shoulder rotation, which are essential for generating power and consistency. Focus on your backswing; you need to extend your arm fully and move the racquet to a more elevated "trophy" position before swinging forward. By initiating the motion with a fluid, rhythmic toss and a complete weight transfer, you will achieve much better pace and accuracy on your flat serve.

In the first video, your toss and rhythm are inconsistent, causing you to rush the swing rather than utilizing a fluid kinetic chain. Focus on extending your arm fully during the toss to achieve a higher contact point, and allow your body to rotate naturally into the serve instead of just using your arm. Practicing a smooth, continuous upward motion will help you generate more power and accuracy in your flat serve.

In the first video, your toss and wind-up are inconsistent, causing your body to lose balance and forcing you to hit the ball with an open, unstable racquet face. To improve, focus on a smooth, rhythmic motion where your toss and racquet arm rise together, and work on staying tall through contact rather than falling toward the side. By keeping your torso stable and following through across your body, you will gain much better control and power on your flat serve.

The beginner’s serve lacks the necessary upward "trophy pose" and fluid kinetic chain, causing the motion to look like a flat arm swing rather than a coordinated throw. To improve, focus on starting your toss higher while simultaneously reaching up with your racquet arm to create a more effective lever for power. Additionally, ensure you are utilizing your legs to drive upward toward the ball rather than just relying on your upper body.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent, causing you to drop your elbow and "slap" at the ball rather than driving through it with a full extension. To improve, focus on a vertical, stable ball toss and practice a smooth, fluid motion that leads with the edge of the racket toward the ball, ensuring your arm fully extends at the point of contact. This will help you generate more power and accuracy while maintaining better control over your serve.

The beginner’s serve lacks the necessary upward extension and full racquet-head speed required for a powerful flat serve, as the motion is too compact and mechanical. To improve, focus on starting with a more pronounced trophy position by reaching fully toward the sky, and ensure you finish the stroke by pronating your wrist through the contact point rather than just swinging your arm forward. This will help you achieve the fluid, whip-like acceleration demonstrated by the expert.

To improve your flat serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous "trophy pose" that maximizes your racquet drop behind your back. Currently, your arm motion is somewhat disjointed; focus on dropping your racquet head deeper toward your back while pushing your chest upward to generate better kinetic energy. This will help you transition from a simple "arm swing" to a powerful, full-body motion that creates the racquet head speed necessary for a true flat serve.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a more fluid, rhythmic motion rather than breaking the movement into distinct, disjointed segments. Ensure your racquet head drops behind your back more completely, allowing for a longer acceleration path into the ball, and practice a consistent, full-range follow-through across your body to generate better power and control.

In your serve, you are currently skipping the essential "trophy pose" and fluid kinetic chain, which limits your power and control. Focus on extending your tossing arm fully upward while rotating your shoulders back before initiating the swing; this will create the necessary coil to drive the racket through the ball rather than just pushing it forward.

Your toss is currently inconsistent, which is causing your body to lunge forward prematurely and prevents a smooth, fluid weight transfer. Focus on keeping your tossing arm extended longer and releasing the ball at a consistent height to ensure you are hitting the ball at the peak of your reach rather than reaching down for it. Additionally, work on fully extending your arm toward the target at the point of contact to increase both the power and the accuracy of your serve.

The beginner's toss is inconsistent and lacks height, which forces an rushed, disconnected swing rather than a fluid, overhead motion. To improve, focus on releasing the ball at full arm extension to create a consistent contact point high above your head, which will allow you to leverage your body weight into the serve instead of just using your arm.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a more complete and fluid motion rather than just arming the ball. You need to incorporate a full wind-up, including a higher toss and a more pronounced "trophy pose" with your elbow raised, to generate the kinetic energy required for a powerful strike. Finally, ensure your body rotates through the ball toward the target after contact, rather than stopping your swing abruptly in front of your body.

To improve your serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous upward motion. Currently, your toss and arm movement are disjointed; try to initiate the racket take-back and the ball toss simultaneously to create better rhythm. Additionally, emphasize a full, high extension through the contact point rather than cutting the swing short, as this will help you achieve the power and depth seen in the expert's technique.

The beginner needs to focus on a smoother, more fluid kinetic chain, as they currently break the motion into disjointed segments rather than a continuous arc. By rotating the shoulders more aggressively during the wind-up and ensuring the racquet drops into a deeper "back scratch" position before acceleration, the player will generate significantly more racquet head speed and power. Additionally, focus on fully extending upward toward the ball at the point of contact rather than cutting the swing short, which will improve both consistency and depth on the serve.

The beginner's swing path is currently restricted, showing a lack of full extension and fluid shoulder rotation compared to the expert's smooth, rhythmic motion. To improve your serve, focus on achieving a fuller "trophy pose" by lifting your racquet higher behind your back and fully extending your arm toward the ball at the point of contact. This will help you generate the necessary racquet head speed and power that the expert demonstrates effortlessly.

The beginner currently uses a stiff, arm-dominated motion that lacks the necessary body rotation and fluid weight transfer required for a powerful serve. To improve, focus on initiating the swing with a rhythmic, full-body coil—tossing the ball higher and allowing your torso to rotate so you can "load" your power before exploding upward into the contact point. Additionally, try to achieve a more relaxed, loose wrist snap at the top to increase racquet head speed and generate natural spin.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent, causing you to reach awkwardly for the ball rather than hitting it at the peak of your extension. Focus on keeping your tossing arm straight longer and aiming for a consistent release point in front of your body. Additionally, try to initiate your swing with a more fluid, rhythmic motion, as your current technique appears stiff and relies too much on your arm rather than the kinetic chain of your legs and core.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a fuller, more fluid overhead motion; currently, your movement is too truncated and lacks the necessary racquet drop to generate power. You should also work on your weight transfer by stepping forward into the court during the swing, rather than keeping your feet stationary, to utilize your entire body's kinetic chain for greater velocity.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a more complete "trophy position" by extending your tossing arm higher and ensuring your racquet drops fully behind your back before accelerating upward. You are currently rushing the motion and limiting your reach; try to prioritize a fluid, full-body extension that allows you to strike the ball at the highest possible point of your reach.

To improve your flat serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous ball toss that reaches a consistent height. Additionally, work on rotating your upper body and fully extending your arm toward the ball at the point of contact to generate more power and downward angle, rather than swinging primarily with your elbow.

The beginner needs to focus on a more fluid, rhythmic motion, as their current swing is segmented and lacks the necessary upward extension to generate power. To improve, work on a smoother "trophy pose" that transitions seamlessly into the ball toss and contact point, rather than starting and stopping the swing. Focusing on this kinetic chain will help you achieve the more fluid, whip-like acceleration seen in the expert's serve.

The beginner's toss is inconsistent and lacks vertical reach, preventing them from making clean contact at the peak of their extension. To improve, focus on releasing the ball higher with a straighter arm, ensuring your racquet head travels fluidly upward into the ball rather than chopping across it. Aim to fully extend your body toward the target, which will add the necessary pace and downward angle required for a consistent flat serve.

To improve your serve, focus on your toss and your preparation: your arm is currently reaching too far across your body rather than fully extending upward toward the contact point. Additionally, try to incorporate a more fluid, rhythmic motion by initiating your swing with a coordinated knee bend and torso rotation, rather than relying solely on your arm.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent, causing you to reach awkwardly for the ball rather than hitting it at the peak of your extension. Focus on keeping your toss arm straight and releasing the ball at eye level to ensure a stable, repeatable contact point. Additionally, try to lead more with your legs and rotate your torso into the shot to generate natural power, rather than relying solely on your arm.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and lacks the necessary height to allow for a proper extension, which is forcing you to strike the ball with a bent arm. To improve, focus on a smooth, upward rhythmic release of the ball and practice full arm extension during your swing to generate more power and racquet-head speed.

To improve your flat serve, focus on a more fluid and continuous ball toss that travels vertically, rather than swinging your racket and toss arm out simultaneously. Additionally, aim to initiate your swing with a more pronounced knee bend and a relaxed, rhythmic motion, allowing for better weight transfer into the court rather than staying static.

The beginner’s serve lacks the necessary upward "trophy pose" and fluid kinetic chain, resulting in a motion that is more of a forward push than a dynamic swing. To improve, focus on initiating your toss and racquet take-back simultaneously to create a rhythmic loop, and ensure you are extending upward toward the ball rather than hitting flat through it. Incorporating this vertical reach will help you achieve the snap and acceleration seen in the expert's serve.

To improve your serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous kinetic chain rather than breaking the motion into disjointed parts. Start by relaxing your arm and ensuring that your racquet head drops behind your back more completely before accelerating upward to the ball, which will help you generate the necessary racquet head speed and power.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent, causing you to reach for the ball awkwardly rather than meeting it at your highest contact point. Focus on keeping your tossing arm extended longer and releasing the ball at eye level to ensure a stable, repeatable platform. Additionally, you need to initiate the serve with a more fluid, rhythmic motion—your current swing feels disjointed, so try to synchronize your knee bend and arm movement to generate more natural power through the kinetic chain.

Your current serve lacks the necessary torso rotation and fluid kinetic chain required for power and consistency. Focus on fully turning your shoulders away from the target during your wind-up and ensuring a smooth, continuous upward motion toward the ball, rather than the fragmented arm motion shown in your swing.

The beginner’s serve lacks the necessary upward extension and fluid kinetic chain, as the racket prematurely crosses the body rather than driving through the contact point. Focus on initiating the serve with a deeper knee bend and a full, upward reach toward the sky, ensuring your racket head stays "on edge" until the final snap at the top of your swing. By prioritizing this vertical motion and a higher contact point, you will achieve the natural pronation and power seen in the expert's technique.

To improve your serve, focus on developing a more fluid kinetic chain by starting with a deeper knee bend and a deliberate, high toss to maximize your reach at the contact point. Your current motion is a bit disjointed; try to synchronize your arm swing and weight transfer so that your body weight moves forward into the ball, rather than staying static. Integrating this full-body motion will help you generate more power and consistency, as seen in the fluid mechanics of the expert's serve.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a more fluid and continuous "trophy pose" by lifting your tossing arm higher and ensuring your racket-side elbow reaches a more pronounced "L" shape behind your head. Your current motion is a bit disjointed; try to initiate the forward swing by driving your legs upward and rotating your hips toward the court to generate more natural power and whip through the contact point.

The beginner should focus on achieving a more fluid, rhythmic motion, as their current swing feels segmented and lacks the proper kinetic chain. Additionally, the beginner needs to integrate a full toss and an extended "trophy position" to generate the upward reach necessary for a proper flat serve, rather than relying on a shortened, purely arm-driven swing.

To improve your serve, you need to focus on a more fluid, continuous motion rather than the segmented, rigid movement you are currently using. In the expert video, notice how the racket head maintains a loose, rhythmic arc; try to relax your wrist and shoulder to allow the racket to "drop" behind your back, which will create the necessary whip and power through the contact point. Additionally, try to incorporate more upward body extension and a fuller weight transfer toward the court to increase the pace and consistency of your serve.

To improve your flat serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous ball toss to build consistent momentum. Additionally, work on fully extending your arm toward the ball at the peak of the toss, rather than catching it prematurely, to maximize your leverage and power through the contact point.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a more fluid and continuous "trophy pose" by lifting your racquet head higher with your elbow leading the motion, rather than pulling it across your body too early. Additionally, emphasize a greater upward extension toward the ball, as you are currently cutting the swing short, which limits both the power and the consistency of your serve.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and your weight transfer is minimal, causing you to reach for the ball rather than driving through it. Focus on keeping your tossing arm extended longer to create a higher contact point and shift your weight from your back foot to your front foot as you initiate the swing to generate more power. Increasing your racquet head speed through a more fluid, explosive motion will help you transition from a "push" to a true serve.

When comparing your serve to the expert, you’ll notice that your arm movement is somewhat stiff and lacks the fluid, full-body kinetic chain required for power. To improve, focus on initiating the swing with a more relaxed, loose arm and incorporate a deeper knee bend to drive upward into the ball rather than just pushing it forward with your shoulder. This will help you transition from a simple arm movement to a dynamic serve that utilizes your entire body for better consistency and pace.

As a coach, I noticed that while you have the right general motion, your preparation lacks the essential fluid "trophy pose" transition. To improve, focus on starting your toss and racquet preparation simultaneously so that your racquet reaches the high, laid-back position behind your head just as your tossing arm fully extends. This will allow you to generate significantly more racket-head speed and a more reliable rhythm, mimicking the expert’s efficient kinetic chain.

The beginner’s serve is currently lacking a proper ball toss and weight transfer, resulting in a static motion that relies only on the arm. To improve, focus on starting your motion with a consistent, vertical toss while shifting your body weight from your back foot to your front foot to generate natural power. You should also aim to reach fully upward toward the ball at the point of contact to ensure you are hitting over the ball rather than just pushing it forward.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a fluid, full-body kinetic chain rather than just relying on your arms. Currently, your motion is segmented; try to initiate the toss and the racquet backswing simultaneously, and ensure you use a full overhead extension to snap the racquet forward at the peak of your contact point. This will help you transition from a simple "arm swing" to a powerful, efficient serving motion.

In the first video, your movement is somewhat disjointed; focus on integrating your leg drive and hip rotation to generate fluid power rather than relying solely on your arm. To improve your mechanics, ensure your racquet drop is deeper behind your back to create a longer swing path and a more consistent point of contact at the apex of your toss.

To improve your serve, you need to focus on a more fluid and continuous kinetic chain rather than breaking the motion into disjointed parts. Notice how the expert incorporates a full, dynamic leg drive and a deep trophy position, which allows for a more powerful racquet-head speed and a cleaner, upward contact point. Practice smoothing out the transition between your toss and your swing to ensure your weight shifts forward, rather than staying static throughout the motion.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a more fluid and continuous motion, as your current swing is somewhat segmented and lacks the necessary pace. You should also work on your trophy pose by elevating your racquet elbow higher and extending your arm more fully to maximize your vertical reach and leverage. Finally, ensure your weight transfer is more aggressive from your back foot to your front foot to generate the power needed for a truly flat serve.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a fluid, continuous motion rather than pausing your racquet behind your back. Your current technique lacks a full "trophy pose" and sufficient racquet drop, which limits your ability to generate power and snap through the ball. Practice a smooth, rhythmic wind-up that allows the racquet to accelerate naturally toward the contact point.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and your body remains too upright, which prevents you from generating necessary leverage. To improve, focus on a more fluid, vertical toss and practice "loading" your legs by bending your knees to create a powerful upward extension into the ball. Finally, ensure your racquet drop is deeper behind your back to create the whip-like acceleration shown in the expert video.

To improve your flat serve, focus on a more fluid and continuous swing path, as your current movement is a bit segmented. Ensure you achieve a higher, more extended ball toss and emphasize a full reach toward the contact point to maximize your power and vertical clearance over the net.

Your ball toss is inconsistent and placed too far behind your head, which forces you to arch your back awkwardly and hinders your ability to drive forward into the ball. Focus on tossing the ball slightly more in front of your body to allow for a natural weight transfer and a more fluid, rhythmic service motion. Additionally, try to extend your arm fully toward the ball at the peak of your toss to create a higher, more consistent contact point.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a fuller, more fluid range of motion; currently, your movement is too truncated and lacks the necessary upward reach. Specifically, work on extending your arm completely toward the contact point to generate more leverage and power, rather than pulling the racket across your body prematurely. Integrating a more dynamic vertical push from your legs will also help you initiate the swing with greater momentum, matching the fluid rhythm seen in the expert's technique.

The beginner's swing path is currently too horizontal, causing the racket to wrap around their body rather than moving through the ball toward the target. To improve, focus on starting your toss higher and driving the racket head upward and outward toward the "12 o'clock" position to create a more vertical, powerful plane of motion. Additionally, try to incorporate a more fluid weight transfer from your back foot to your front foot to generate momentum rather than relying solely on your arm.

The beginner needs to focus on a more fluid, rhythmic wind-up; currently, the movement is a bit segmented, whereas the expert uses a continuous motion that generates better momentum. Additionally, work on incorporating a more pronounced leg drive and full shoulder rotation, as the expert effectively shifts their weight forward to power the serve rather than relying primarily on the arms.

To improve your serve, focus on developing a more complete kinetic chain by tossing the ball higher and reaching fully upward with your arm at contact rather than swinging horizontally across your body. Your current motion is too abbreviated; try to incorporate a deliberate "trophy pose" where your elbow is high and your racket drops behind your back, allowing you to drive upward and forward into the ball for greater power and consistency.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a more fluid, overhead toss and reaching fully upward with your arm at the peak of the ball's trajectory, as seen in the expert's motion. Currently, your arm motion is quite compressed; try to extend your racquet arm completely to maximize your leverage and power on the serve.

In the first video, your movement is somewhat disjointed because you are relying almost entirely on your arm strength rather than a fluid, kinetic chain. To improve, focus on initiating the swing with a more synchronized leg drive and torso rotation, which will help you generate natural power instead of just pushing the ball forward. Additionally, try to achieve a more relaxed, full follow-through across your body to ensure you aren't "chopping" at the ball, which will provide you with much better control and consistency.

The beginner should focus on developing a more fluid and continuous service motion, as their current swing is segmented and lacks the proper rhythm seen in the expert's example. To improve, work on a smoother weight transfer and a more relaxed "trophy pose" transition; the expert demonstrates a better coiling of the torso, which generates greater racket-head speed and power through the contact point.

In your serve, you are currently pushing the ball rather than utilizing a full kinetic chain, which lacks the necessary fluid acceleration found in the expert's motion. To improve, focus on starting with a more relaxed, loose grip and prioritize a full, rhythmic wind-up that allows the racket to drop behind your back before exploding upward to contact the ball at its highest point. Finally, ensure your weight transfers forward during the swing, as the expert demonstrates a more dynamic body rotation that translates into significantly more power and accuracy.

To improve your serve, focus on developing a more fluid kinetic chain by starting your motion with a proper trophy pose where the racket reaches up high behind your head, rather than swinging it directly across your body. You also need to shift your weight forward into the court during the swing to generate power, instead of remaining flat-footed. Prioritize these mechanics to create a more efficient, upward-striking motion that mimics the smooth, full-extension arc seen in the expert's technique.

The beginner needs to focus on a more fluid and continuous kinetic chain, as their current motion is segmented and lacks the proper full-body rotation seen in the expert. Ensure that the racquet drop is deeper behind the back before accelerating upward, rather than pushing the ball forward, to generate the necessary head speed and natural follow-through across the body.

To improve your serve, focus on achieving a fluid, continuous motion; your current technique pauses significantly at the top of the swing, which prevents you from generating necessary racquet head speed. Additionally, practice your ball toss to ensure it is consistently placed in front of your body, as this will allow you to transition your weight forward into the court during the swing, similar to the expert’s efficient movement.

The beginner is currently pushing the ball rather than utilizing a fluid, overhead swing motion. To improve, focus on starting your racquet in a high "trophy" position behind your head and initiating the swing by leading with your elbow to create a whipping action through the ball, which will help you generate much-needed pace and proper topspin. Additionally, ensure you are fully extending your arm at the point of contact to maximize reach and leverage.

In the first video, your toss is inconsistent and lacks the necessary vertical extension, causing you to contact the ball too low and lose potential power. To improve, focus on a smooth, synchronized motion where your tossing arm and racket arm move upward together, ensuring you reach high to make contact at the peak of your extension. Maintaining a more fluid transition from your loading phase into the ball will help you achieve the clean, driving pace seen in the expert's serve.

To improve your serve, focus on the fluidity of your motion and your body alignment; your current swing is somewhat segmented, whereas the expert maintains a smooth, connected kinetic chain that transfers power from the legs through the torso. Additionally, work on your ball toss consistency and increasing your shoulder rotation, as the expert demonstrates a more pronounced "loading" phase that leads to a more powerful and controlled follow-through.

The beginner's serve lacks a fluid kinetic chain, as the arm motion is disjointed from the body's rotation and the racquet head drops inconsistently during the backswing. To improve, focus on starting with a more relaxed grip and ensuring a synchronized "trophy pose" where your legs, hips, and shoulders load together before exploding upward into the ball. Prioritize a higher, more consistent toss location to allow for a full, smooth extension of the hitting arm toward the target.

To improve your flat serve, focus on achieving a more fluid, rhythmic motion rather than breaking the movement into distinct, disjointed parts. In the second video, the expert maintains a continuous kinetic chain—the weight transfer, toss, and racket drop happen as one integrated motion—which generates significantly more power and racket-head speed than your current static approach. Work on synchronizing your toss with a smooth, unbroken swing path to transition from a tentative movement to a powerful, explosive serve.
